steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a pronoun–antecedent agreement and subject–verb agreement question. Strip the dash clause (“water and electricity are two well-known examples”) to expose the true subject: “a given industry.” That antecedent is singular, so the blank needs a singular pronoun with a singular verb.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — they often consist: They is a plural pronoun; it does not agree with the singular antecedent “a given industry.” Pronoun–antecedent agreement error. ✗
  • Choice B — this often consists: This is a singular demonstrative pronoun and consists is singular, so the number agreement is correct, but this typically points to a nearby noun or idea rather than referring back to a noun as a pronoun substitute. The SAT prefers it when the antecedent is a singular count noun like “industry.” ✗
  • Choice C — these often consist: These is a plural demonstrative pronoun; it does not agree with the singular antecedent “a given industry.” Pronoun–antecedent agreement error. ✗
  • Choice D — it often consists: It is the standard singular third-person pronoun that refers back to “a given industry,” and consists is the correct singular verb form. Both pronoun–antecedent agreement and subject–verb agreement are satisfied. ✓
That matches Choice D — it often consists.
